All right, JB in the house here for your daily motivation slash skills training. I want to talk about big picture questions. It's your bonus module here. Your bonus lesson, I should say, right? Listen, of all those questions, the reason I single those three out is those three are magical. I've been doing this. Well, I've been using that strategy for like 25 years already. Seriously, I'm like, you know, what do you like about your current source? What do you not like about your current source? Okay. And if I can design something that was, you know, just directly for you, what would that product look like? Three questions. Tell me what you like about the current broker or the product you're using. The reason, again, the reason that we ask that question first, we don't just say, so what's, what do you not like what you're using? Well, if you do that, you come off as being very disingenuous, like you're shop shooting. Of course, anyone can point hole and plug play. This is wrong with that. That's, you know, you don't want to be that guy or that girl. That's really classless to say, so what's wrong with what do you like about you're using right now? It just sounds like it's too much on the nose. You know, it's funny, like when we were writing the Wolf of Wall Street script, right? You know, there was certain things and we were looking for the right ending and I said, hey, how about like, you know, I was doing a lot of charity work in Africa. I was like, why don't we end it with me doing charity work in Africa? You know, great full cycle redemption. And then they looked at it and they said, Joe, it's so, it's so stupid in a sense that it's so on the nose. It's you're telling someone like how they should, you're redeemed. Don't do that. How about we have you selling like and back on top as a salesman? That's the irony. That's not that because it's like it leaves the moral question open about sales, about what I did. See, when something is too on the nose, people feel like you're shoving it down your throat. You're moralizing. The reason Scorsese is so brilliant at what he does is because he doesn't tell you how you should feel. He just paints a picture for you and lets you feel how you want to feel based on events. He doesn't insult your intelligence. When you say to someone, so what don't you like about your current product or service or broker? And then when you give him a pitch five minutes later, you tell him exactly what that was. You insulted their intelligence. You get it? It's pretty out on your figure. It's pretty obvious you're insulting someone's intelligence. They've been nature. We don't do that with this straight line. We're much more careful on that. And it's not like it's evil or not. You will just stupid. We're smarter than that. So I say, so tell them what do you like about the current product? You have. Now I'm not looking to find the old, you know, get accolades on the other guy or the price. I'm not going to do it. I don't want to make him say, oh, it's this, but I might need to do that. Uh-huh. And also I want to know what features are important to him, what benefits he values. So when I make my presentation, I'm sure to include those. And then when I say and then logically and what don't you like, what, you know, what is it that drives you crazy? But what's worse was what more waste your time. Now I've earned the right to ask the second question. You understand? I've earned the right to ask the second question without the first one. I haven't earned the right. I'm too much on the nose. I'm revealed as being a sharp shooter and a guy just wants to close the sale at all costs. I'm cutting it. I'm slick and it's not how you want to be perceived. That's not being an expert. That's being cutting and slick. No good. But when you ask those two together, you get in the yang. Perfect. And that leads, of course, to the third one, which is, and if you could design this for yourself, tell me what would it look like? It's an obvious third question and they'll tell you. Um, it's interesting though, it's like, I never doubted though from here, my voice is like kind of, I was crying. Um, I never doubted for a second that I met what was going on that I would do this today, because I always stand up and rise up for what I got to do. You know, I do whatever it takes to get things done. That doesn't mean you don't have to, you don't feel the feelings and the hurt and the pain, right? But as an adult, we have to get ourselves to do the things we got to do. Even we don't feel like doing it. I wanted to crawl up and bed and just, you know, and think about all the great times that happened to my dad, you know, but he kind of made me into the man I had today, at least partially, right? And he was there for me in those crazy days. So, you know, my dad would respect that I'm working. I'm doing my thing, helping people get rich, helping people close sales and having a great time while I'm doing it. You know, anyway, that's why I think I kind of flubbed that one line next. I just know, I know the patterns so well. As I was saying, is that you've earned the right now to ask that question. You've now really, truly do have the keys to the kingdom. Cause when they tell you, you know, well, these are, this is, if I could design it for myself, this is exactly what it would look like. I mean, there's your sales presentation in reverse, but from those three questions alone, you have everything you need to know to close a deal. Now, obviously there's a lot more than that because you need to have the bigger picture questions that come before that, the less invasive questions. You know, you have to get to that. But at the end of the day, that really crystallizes what straight line prospect thing is all about. And that's why I had that lesson last made it a bonus lesson. It was that valuable. That's it. Anyway, guys, tough week for me. Um, I love you all. Cherish your family seriously, playful out. And also, you know what, don't sweat the small stuff. My dad, he was a master at sweating the small stuff. No one better in the world in a crisis. My dad loved a good crisis, but he drove himself crazy and sweated the small stuff. And he never refused, he refused essentially to use anything that even approached influence and persuasion. And he struggled his whole life financially because of it until I came along and then my ability transcended his inability. And he worked for me in the rest of his three. You saw the movie, but we go through life once, make it count. Make every day count. Love your family, but work hard, be a provider and don't live your life.
